video_title: How to See a Future Worth Living In with Rob Hopkins | TGS 224
transcript: There are many, many different futures, but  there are some that result from us doing   everything we could possibly have done.  And actually, the reality is that all the   elements of what that looks like already  exist somewhere. Like William Gibson said,   "The future's already here. It's  just not evenly distributed." You can go to Utrecht in Holland and sit by  the train station and 40,000 bicycles will   cycle past you. It's insane. You go to Liège in  Belgium, where they're building a food belt in   a way that has just transformed the economy of  that city. You know, everything that we need to   know already exists somewhere, and there's  a lot of research around how when people   imagine the world in that way, it increases  their sense of agency to create that world Today I'm joined by longtime friend and  fellow systems analyst Rob Hopkins for   a discussion about how the imagination can  help us move from paralysis to action in the   face of the complexity of the more than human  predicament. Rob Hopkins is a co-founder of   Transition Town Totnes and Transition Network,  a global grassroots movement dedicated to   building community resilience, especially  to meet ecological energy and food needs. He is the author of many books, including most  recently, How to Fall in Love with the Future:   A Time Traveller's Guide to Changing the  World, and also was the host of the now   concluded podcast From What If to What  Next. An Ashoka fellow and holder of a   doctorate from the University of Plymouth,  Rob's work has been featured in TED Global,   BBC Radio, and the French film  Demain, which means future. His collaborative music project with artist  Mr. Kit, Field Recordings from the Future,   is being developed as a live touring show. In  today's conversation, Rob makes the case that we   are facing a crisis of imagination, and that our  collective inability to vividly envision radically   different and better futures might inadvertently  lead us down bleak or unrealistic paths. Drawing on neuroscience, storytelling,  and a lifetime of grassroots work,   he explores why creative visions of the  future can be a causal force for change.   He shares the tools and practices he uses  to cultivate what he calls longing at scale,   a deep felt sense of what we're  building toward, not just running from. This includes the concept of evidence-based  dreaming, where key events happening in the   world are grounded within coherent sensory  and emotionally alive pictures, showing us new   possibilities. While Rob and I have diverging  perspectives in some key areas, we share a   commitment to explore the full range of responses  to the unfolding more than human predicament. And actually, this might be one of the most  important aspects of this episode and this   channel more broadly. Even among the people  that we are highly aligned with in terms of   what we value for the future, there will always  be pieces we disagree on, and listening to each   other to find common ground is one of the  best ways to build and expand relationships,   which will ultimately form the foundation for any  responses to the unfolding predicament around us. With that, please welcome Rob Hopkins.  Rob Hopkins, welcome to the program Nate,   so lovely to see you again. Been far too long,  my friend Well, it's at least since 2009,   which I have incredible memories. Um,  the former head of the CIA came up to   me at that conference and shook my  hand, and then he gave me a hug. He put his whiskey down and gave  me a hug And I remember your, like,   all these high-level f- intelligence and oil  people, and then you get on the stage, and you   were so soft-spoken, and you could've heard a pin  drop, and I, I remember that. That was the first   public talk I had ever given, actually. Um- Oh,  wow Yeah And did, did- didn't you have a thing   in the middle where you had, like a, a sort of an  animated thing that just screamed at everybody? Yes, the Jack Nicholson, like, sc- I scared  everyone. Uh, yeah. That was 2009. Man, uh,   life, life, uh, goes fast It does. But then  I saw you again in 2013 in a hotel lobby in   Milwaukee. We hung out and had a catch-up I'm  known to be in hotel lobbies in Milwaukee No,   but yeah, that's right. So that was 13  years ago Yeah Um, great to see you. As I said, uh, off camera, you haven't aged a  day in, in those years I wish. Well, you're very   kind Yeah. So you are, um Probably one of the  most active voices in the world of grassroots   change-making, as well as, uh, a longtime  colleague of mine. But for those who don't yet   know your work, you co-founded the Transition  Town Movement, now called Transition Network,   which began in Totnes, England, and  has since spread around the world. So maybe we start with you, uh, briefly  describing what the Transition Network   concept is and talk about what you're specifically  transitioning to. Great question. So yeah. Um,   so the Transition Movement started in the small  town that I live in the southwest of England,   which you... th- whose name  you pronounced correctly. Thank you very much. Uh, the town of  Totnes. And we are a town of about 9,000   people in the southwest of England.  And in 2005, 2006, a bunch of us,   uh, came together. I had been living in Ireland  before. I was a permaculture teacher. I'd set   up the first two-year full-time permaculture  course in the world, I think, at that point. And I moved to Totnes, and I met some people  who were interested in similar things,   who'd been thinking about, "Okay, we  have a climate emergency. We have an   energy security emergency. There are  the things that we know we need to   do as individuals. There are the things we're  waiting for the... we need governments to do. But what's the other bit? What's the bit that  we could do if we organized with the people   around us?" We had a, a saying, which I still  see referenced all over the place, that said,   "If we try and do this on our own, it'll  be too little. If we wait for governments,   it'll be too late. But if we get  together with the people around us,   it might just be enough, and  it might just be in time." And initially, it was really something that was  an experiment for our town. We had no expectation,   no imagining even that it would be of any interest  to anywhere else at all. This was early kind   of internet days, so then I had a blog called  Transition Culture. We had a kind of a wiki page,   if anyone can remember wikis, where we were  sort of sharing early ideas as they evolved. And, um, and it kind of just took off,  and we were doing stuff in Totnes,   but it took off in that there were also, uh...  Within six months, we had so much interest from   people all over the world. This before, was before  there were any books published. We had people from   Brazil and New Zealand and the US and wherever  writing to us and saying, "What is this thing? How do we do it?" And so we had to create  Transition Network, which is now called   Transition Network International, as  the support organization to support,   network, inspire, and train that movement  as it grew. And it really became a sort of   self-organizing experiment around what's  the bit that, that communities can do. So now it's spread to about 50 different  countries. You'll find thousands of,   of transition groups, and they do all sorts of  different things from different local economy   kind of projects through to community  food stuff, community energy projects,   community energy companies. Uh, even  now in, in Totnes after nearly 20 years,   we have a project where the community is becoming  its own housing developer, meeting the affordable   housing gap that nobody else is, is filling, so  coming together as a community s- to, to do that. Somebody once called Transition hope with  its sleeves rolled up, which I really,   really loved. And one of the best things I  ever read about the Transition movement was   written by a guy called Luigi Russi, who came  for a year as a student to Schumacher College,   which used to be, uh, in Totnes until  it sadly closed a couple of years ago. And he, uh, he ba-- he came for a year, and  he just got involved with everything. Every   meeting you went to, Luigi was there. Every  meeting, he was there helping putting out the   chairs and then washing up the teacups at the  end. He just threw himself into everything.   And then he wrote this beautiful little book  called Everything Gardens reflecting on that. And one of the things that really... It's  not often that I read things about Transition   where I'm like, "God, that's..." Where I  underline loads of stuff. You know? "Wow,   that's really interesting." And he said that, that  academics always love to look at the Transition   movement like a thing that you can sort of pin  to a board and then look at under a microscope. And he said what's far more interesting is  the Transition moving, the way that Transition   moves and absorbs new influences and changes  and evolves and adapts over time. And for me,   that's always been one of its real strengths, that  it, that it's always changing and adapting, uh,   and evolving. Well, when, when did you found it? 20 years ago, right? Almost? 2006. September  2006 was the official launch, yeah. Yeah. So how,   how has it changed? Well, I think it's important  to say that I-- so the last three or four years,   I, I don't work for Transition Network anymore.  I stepped out to focus on this work I'm doing   now around imagination and time and different  things, which we'll come on to talk about. So for a really, really up-to-date update  on Transition, you need to get somebody on   from Transition Network. But I would say that  it's, um- What's been fascinating to me was   that when we started it, we were very clear that  transition was something that communities did. It   was a piece of kind of social infrastructure  building equipment for people working at the   local scale who wanted to build resilience  in the face of what we all saw coming. So when we started it, we were very clear  this was a kind of a piece of, of social   design infrastructure tool for communities to,  to take, uh, and get on with. And, and that was   the model. So in the Transition Handbook,  the first book on, on it, it had these 12,   what we called the 12 steps of transition, because  just basically people said, "What are you doing?" And we said, "Well, we're doing this and we're  doing some of that." And oh, there's 12 of them,   and we quite liked the kind of metaphor of it  being like a 12-step program for breaking our,   our addiction to oil. But then I remember in about  2013, I got-- uh, 2014, I got approached by, um, a   mayor who in the northeast of France, in a little  town called Ungersheim, called Jean-Claude Mensch. And Jean-Claude Mensch said, um, uh, "Will you  come to Ungersheim? We're doing something really   interesting, and we'd love to see what you  think of it." And so I traveled to Ungersheim,   and he had seen the film, uh, the film we had  made about Transition called In Transition 2.0,   which listeners can still find on YouTube. And he showed it to all of his team in this  municipality in that part of France, and he said,   uh, "Let's do that, all of that."  And so the municipality created this,   this amazing 21 actions for the 21st  century, uh, and they were doing amazing   selection of things around energy and food and  building, and it was-- absolutely blew my mind. And it-- but, but this was... So was this  transition or was this not transition?   Transition was something that communities did, and  here was a mayor who would-- so 'cause in France,   mayors, if mayors decide they're  gonna do something really ambitious,   they can do really ambitious things,  right? So he's like, "We're gonna do this." And, uh, and it was the most extraordinary  thing to see. So that's kind of started to   shift then how we thought about it.  Well, who cares really? I mean, that,   that- Exactly. ... with my work on The Great  Simplification, it's changing the initial   conditions of the future. And whatever you  label it or whatever, I, I mean, who cares? Well, exactly. And so I think that's then the  w-where-- so we did a piece of work about four   years ago in Transition Network where we tried to  kind of map how this stuff is spreading and how   this stuff is moving, and that's the interesting  bit. So we have-- So now when I go to France, uh,   there are cities like Grenoble where they have had  an ecological administration now for two terms,   and they are really enthusiastic about transition,  and they're doing transition at the city scale. And the mayor of Grenoble says to me, uh,  "Rob, uh, we love transition. We're doing   transition on the city scale. We don't have any  transition groups, uh, in Grenoble. How could we   start them?" And I'm like" Well, maybe you don't  need them. You know, maybe if... They'll come if,   if, if it's right." And then sometimes you just  see where, where the idea has kind of gone to. So, so for example, in France, uh, I was watching  a video that you made where you were talking about   this idea that you had for a film that was  about time travel, and you were saying, well,   and you were talking about the day after  or the horror-horrific nuclear war film,   and you were saying, "Well, I don't  think we could do this anymore." You couldn't make a film now that everybody  would sit down and watch. In France, they,   they kind of had a film that everybody sat  down and watched. There was a film that came   out in twenty fifteen called Demain, which means  tomorrow, uh, which, which I was in talking about   local currencies. And it was a, and it was an  extraordinary film because what it did was it... Most films about climate change are like, you  know, the inconvenient truth kind of model,   where it's ninety-eight percent of how screwed we  are, and then the little happy chapter on the end   that goes, "But hey, we could drive slower," by  which time everyone's left the cinema in tears,   you know. That film flipped it, and they  did the problem in the first two minutes,   and the rest of the film was,  "Let's go and see solutions. We'll go and see this, we'll go  and see this." It was, it was a,   it was a phenomena in a way that's impossible  to imagine anywhere else. Showed in mainstream   cinemas for months and months and was full  every night. It was shown in boardrooms.   It was shown all over the place. It was kind  of extraordinary. And I think that film had   a huge impact because it was like-- it was, it  wasn't specifically about the transition model,   but it was about this idea of, the  idea of the ecological transition. And it started with them saying, "Let's go  to Totnes and find out how to do it." And   so sometimes these ideas find really  interesting routes into a society,   I think. Does Totnes look considerably different  today than it did in two thousand and six? Uh,   I think you need to know where to  look, and I think that there are many,   many projects that have come, and some  of them were kind of short-term things. Some of them have left a real sort of legacy.  Uh, as I said, there are now 39 affordable   homes for people in housing need, very energy  efficient homes that are now currently being   built. First families will move in in September.  We have a community energy company which has, uh,   uh, which has many, many members and  installed lots of renewable energy. We have an amazing event every year called the  Local Entrepreneur Forum, where we've built a   culture where people are happy to invest in  innovative new ideas. Happens every year,   five people pitch their ideas. There's many  businesses that exist now because of that. Uh,   lots of... I mean, there's, there's  many, many different projects. I always kind of think, you know, that actually  also when you look back since we started the   transition movement, in those years, 30% of  all the CO2 ever released by humanity has   been released during that time. So on one hand,  you kind of think, well, we, we absolutely have   not succeeded. But then I also think it's really  important not to beat ourselves up about that. I always say, if you'd given us the amount  of money that oil and gas companies have   given disinformation campaigns over that  time, uh, it could have been kind of, we,   we could have achieved a hell of a lot more  doing stuff with virtually no resources. I   think one of the things about transition  is that it is an incredible now body of   learning and practices from all around the  world in different contexts and settings. Was the impetus primarily a climate, uh, one,  or was it a post-growth, how do we prepare for,   um, the biophysical, uh, haircut that's ahead?  That's a great term, the biophysical haircut. I,   I don't think I've ever used that term.  I like that. It just came to mind. I like   that. 'Cause subconsciously I know that I need  a haircut, so it's, it's in the back of my mind. Um, I think when we started, we said that  it was underpinned and driven by the twin   drivers of climate change and peak oil. We were  very-- we, we very much were kind of emerging in   parallel with the peak oil kind of movement and,  you know, I, reading The Party's Over and watching   The End of Suburbia were kind of a big kicks up  the backside for me in terms of starting that. But then as time went on, I think, you  know, peak oil kind of fell away as a,   as a primary driver, and climate change became  a lot more urgent and pressing. Um, I think,   I also think now if we were starting again, one  of the focuses would also be on uh, on f- on the   rise of fascism. There's, I like the connection  with, with, with fascism in this work as well. But it's very much in that kind of, I  guess transition is very much rooted in   the kind of response to a sort of limits to  growth, post-growth kind of approach. Yeah.   And, and that this is starting to happen, and it  wasn't... I guess it started to happen, uh, in a   dress rehearsal sort of way in 2008 and 2009, the,  the financial crisis, and then again in COVID. And now the Strait of Hormuz is a lot  of the things we've been talking about   are- Yeah ... unfolding in real time. Yeah.  And I, I know that Transition Network is,   is centralized in, in, uh, England and Europe,  where there is some economic contractions and   energy price increases in recent years and  recent days So how, how have geopolitical   events been affecting Transition Network's  goals and ability to accomplish change? Um, is... Does it make people more receptive  to hearing this different narrative about the   future? Or, uh, what has been your experience?  Granted, you're, you're no longer with, uh,   Transition Network, but what do you think? Well,  I always remember that every time the oil price   went above a certain amount, the phone would start  ringing and journalists would start ringing up. Mm. You know, we, we were like the kind  of people to ring when the oil price went   above $100 a barrel. Some journalist  would be writing an article. "Ah,   what if there were people out there who'd been  thinking about this stuff in advance? What might   that look like? Oh, let's ring Transition  Network." So, so we, you know, we had... There was a lot of that. I always, I always  remember... So when we wrote the Transition   Handbook, we were ma- very much making the  argument that our dependency on imported   fossil fuels, our dependency on, on long supply  chains for food and other resources represented   a key vulnerability and a key fragility, and  that actually that, that lack of resilience,   one of the things that inspired us  was that in the year 2000 in the UK,   all the lorry drivers went on strike and  it became very clear that we only had three   days' worth of food in the supermarkets at, at  any time, and all the previous infrastructure   that would've supported us, sort of local  supply chains had largely been dismantled. And, um, so in the Transition Handbook,  we set out all that stuff, you know. And   sometimes I get-- I still do talks and people  say, "Ah, Rob, you're very naive." And it,   and it gets me because I always say,  "Do you know, in 2008 we wrote a book,   right? And we said, we said that our dependency  on fossil fuels was a key vulnerability." Across the European Union between 2008 and 2022,  the European Union sent Russia 18 billion euros a   month, a month for natural gas. And all that time  we were told there was no money to decarbonize,   there was no money to build a re- a,  a, a kind of a renewable economy. There   was no money to do the stuff that the  transition movement was talking about. And then, oh, what a surprise. Well,  we've sent all this money to Russia,   and then we see what happens with it,  you know. And so now we're seeing what,   what's happening in, in, in the Strait  of Hormuz and, and, uh, it's like, oh,   you know, in Ireland recently, the, they,  they had a load of farmers drove tractors   into the center of Dublin to block the  city to protest about high energy prices. I'm like, "How have you not seen this  coming, really? You had a, you had a,   you had a, a, a, a big dry run in 2008,  in 2022, and you're still saying like,   'Why did nobody tell us about this?'" That's,  well, if you remember my presentation in Cork   in 2009, which of course you don't,  um- Apart from the screaming bit Americans have two modes, uh, complacency and  panic. Yeah. And, you know, humans have two   modes, uh, complacency and panic. So you  mentioned it earlier, but let me ask you,   uh, explicitly, knowing what you know now and  seeing the Superorganism nearly doubling in size   in the last 20 years and all the things,  is there anything you would've tried to   do differently with Transition Network from the,  from the start if you were starting again today? Yeah. I think what I would've done is I would  have, uh, and I've no idea how I would've done   this, but I would've convened the smartest,  richest people that I knew into a room,   and I would've said Can you give me the ideas  and some initial investment that means that this   movement can be self-supporting and self-funding  to the scale of ambition that it needs to be? You know, maybe, uh, you know, maybe  that they would've put some money in   that would've been boo-- You know, th-th-there  are smart people who can do that, you know,   because we-- this is a ma-- We've always  been sort of scrabbling around for bits   here and bits there and bits here, and I  think to be able to have an organization   that can support itself and fund itself in  that way would've been pretty transformative. Is it too late to start that now? Never too late.  It's never too late. You know, there's a, there's   the thing that's frus- There, there's so much  money out there, and it's all-- And, uh, I mean,   not all of it, you know, and, and there are  some, there are some funders who are starting   to get this stuff now, and we have a bit of  funding for some of them from a project that   I'm doing now that I will talk a bit more about  later, but it's nowhere near where we need to be. Well, it's digital claims on biophysical  and ecological reality, and this is it.   You have to decide how to direct those claims  towards life and towards a, a world where your   children and grandchildren-- I mean, yeah, you  wanna keep your status and your convenience, but,   uh, if, if everything goes towards the  Mordor economy, uh, it's not gonna work   for anyone d-d-- irrespective of how  money digits you have in the bank. So yeah, I agree with you there. Yeah, this is the  time. Now is the time. You know, anyone who's been   sitting there thinking, "Well, there might come  a day when actually this money's not gonna be   much use, and I'm gonna f-- and I should..." This  is- Yeah ... this is it. We're there now. Well,   with the exception of AI, we gotta invest and  make more money, and then we can donate to the- uh, saving ecological civilization. Yeah.  Yeah. So, um, a major thread running through   your early work and your more recent work is  the idea that we are suffering from, uh, yeah,   the meta-crisis, but parallel to that, a failure  of cultural imagination- Yeah ... that we as   human beings and as groups of humans struggle to  envision radically different and better futures. And I sensed that emanating from you even almost  20 years ago. So can you expand on this idea and   explain the link between cultural ideas and, and  actual action? Yeah. So in about tw- early 2018,   there was a, there was about two months where  I found that I read articles by George Monbiot,   Naomi Klein, Amitav Ghosh, and a couple  of other people, and in all of them,   the same phrase appeared, where they said  climate change is a failure of the imagination. And it kind of, it kind of stayed with me. Like  I, I... And then they would move on and talk,   be talking about other things, and I was left  there like the kid at the back of the class going,   "Ooh, ooh, ooh," like, "What did you mean?  What did you mean by that?" You know? And   then I read this study that was written by a,  a researcher in the US called Kyung Hee Kim. It was called The Creativity Crisis, where  she looked at something called the Torrance   Test for Creative Thinking, which is  a kind of a, a measure of divergent   thinking that it's the closest thing we  have to an imagination test, basically,   which has been done with huge data sets  of people all the way back till the 1950s. Her conclusion was that imagination and  IQ had risen together until the mid-'90s,   at which point IQ kept rising and imagination  started to decline. This, when it was published,   it made the front page of Newsweek magazine  in 2010, and, uh, there was a big... It,   it evoked a whole lot of soul-searching. People  said, "What does this mean for economic growth?" And, "What does this mean for Hollywood?"  Neither of which I give a shit about really,   but actually then it was... But  for me it was like, okay, well,   climate change fundamentally demands  that we reimagine everything. That's,   that's the, that's the, the challenge, and  that's the gift that it brings, right? I'm,   I'm sorry, I have to interrupt you there because  number one, I'm wondering why that happened. Maybe she knew, and you can share  it. Yeah. And number two, I wonder if throttling down one's imagination of negative  imagination things like learning about climate   change and a three-degree Celsius world where we  lose half the species and all the other possible   colorful trajectories in our mind, that it's  almost a self-preservation to shrink that,   and when we shrink that, we shrink the positive  imagination's, uh, sort of, um, machinery as well. Maybe. Maybe. I mean, so, so in her paper  she, she attributed it to three things.   She said it was due to the decline of  free unstructured play in the lives of   our children. Okay. That makes sense. It was due  to the rise of screens in our lives, and it was   due to the rise of testing in schools. I then  set out to write, uh, From What If to What... From What Is to What If, and f- and I think  there are n- a number of other things that,   that adjoin that as well. I think we also would  say that the spending less and less time in the   natural world is a key cause for that. I think  also, uh, there's a very clear link for me with,   with, uh, s- with stress and trauma and  anxiety and the release of cortisol. That when we have more cortisol  in our system, the hippocampus,   the part of our brain where that comes from,  shrinks by up to 20%, and when that happens,   the future kind of contracts and starts  to disappear. Why? Why? The hippocampus   is responsible for imagination of- Is where  the imagination and the memory both fire from. Imagination and memory- Oh ... are largely  the same process, right? And the hippocampus   is the part of your brain that is especially  vulnerable to cortisol. So people, for example,   who have post-traumatic stress disorder, they  can sometimes have a p- hippocampus about 20%   smaller than average, and that means that the  future, and the, the future starts to disappear. People get stuck in the present and stuck in the  past. And I feel like we've really created a kind   of a cortisol economy right now, where, where so  many people feel in a state of precariousness that   actually that's one of the things that shuts  down our ability to imagine the future. So   is cortisol via the hic- hippocampus steepening  our discount rates, like we only care about the   present and the future is too hard to care about,  or is it actually inhibiting our imagination? Because I can just sit outside, uh, and look at  the birds and the clouds and the trees, and my   mind goes on a journey, um, of imagining different  things, and I always have my little, uh, recorder   with me in case I have an idea for a Frankly. And  yet I h- also have a steep discount rate that I'm,   I'm thinking about the present, so can  you unpack the difference between those? Is it- Yeah, I mean, I would say firstly  there's, there's, there's a recognition   that you're sitting outside and looking at the  birds and the trees and imagining is a real   privilege these days. Like so many- Mm-hmm ...  people never get the time to do that. Like that,   that ability to live an imaginative life in  that way is not evenly distributed at all. Albert Einstein always said he got his  best ideas when he rode his bicycle in   the forest. Yeah. But people spend less  and less and less and less time in the   natural world now. I think both of  those things are true, actually,   that actually we are, we, we are losing both of  those things. I have so many questions on this. So I, I get that one of your  core philosophies now is,   and I actually agree with this, but I also have  a, a, a parallel, um, construction as well,   that if we have all the conversations about the  future are all doom and gloom on all the things,   we have short-circuited our biggest skill as  humans, which is to imagine and converse and   discuss and build, uh, something  creative to meet our challenges. I fully agree with you on that, and I'm  moving more in that direction myself. However,   big asterisk, we don't want that imagination to  be fairy dust and unicorns from a biophysical   perspective, so it has to be grounded  in reality. But let me ask you this.   When we're just discussing imagination, the  ability of a human mind to be creative and   imagine, and when we're talking about the  future, there are a lot of scary things,   uh, about the future that is  unfolding, you know, by the day. Tell me how Well, I'll just parse it as  positive imagination and negative scary   imagination. How, how do you rectify the,  the difference between those two things?   I think it's really important to say when I, w-  when I, when we talk about positive imagining,   like you say, it's not unicorns and  fairy dust. You know, there's a,   there was a film that came out a few years  ago that I'm sure many of your listeners   will have seen called 2040, an Australian film  about the future made by Damon Gameau, Gameau? I can't remember what his surname was. Yeah.  Damon's been on the program, yeah. Okay. So   when he wrote about that film, some of that film  I loved, some of that film not quite so much,   but I thought it was a really brave, brilliant  thing to do. a discussion about how the imagination can  help us move from paralysis to action in the
face of the complexity of the more than human  predicament. Rob Hopkins is a co-founder of
Transition Town Totnes and Transition Network,  a global grassroots movement dedicated to
building community resilience, especially  to meet ecological energy and food needs.

concluded podcast From What If to What  Next. An Ashoka fellow and holder of a
